name: Halloween Effect / Sell in May Seasonal Switch
author: QuantConnect (Bouman & Jacobsen 2002)
description: Holds SPY (or equity ETF) from November 1 through April 30 ('winter season') and switches to AGG/TLT or cash from May 1 through October 31 ('summer season'). Exploits the well-documented Halloween Effect: equity returns are significantly higher in the November–April half-year than in May–October, documented across 108 years of US data and 37 countries. Bouman & Jacobsen (2002) published in the American Economic Review.

name: Turn of Quarter Window Dressing Effect
author: QuantConnect (Lakonishok et al. 1991)
description: Holds SPY during the window-dressing period: last 3 trading days + first 2 trading days of each calendar quarter (20 trading days/year, ~8% of days). Institutional portfolio managers systematically buy recent winners before quarter-end reporting to 'dress' their portfolios, creating concentrated demand. The effect is strongest at quarter-end (March, June, September, December) vs. month-end, and is magnified by reinvestment inflows at quarter start. Lakonishok, Shleifer, Thaler & Vishny (1991) Journal of Finance.

name: January Small-Cap Tax-Loss Selling Reversal
author: QuantConnect (Keim 1983, Reinganum 1983)
description: Holds IWM (Russell 2000 small-cap ETF) in January and reverts to SPY (or cash) for the remaining 11 months. Exploits the 'January Effect': institutional tax-loss selling pressure in December systematically depresses small-cap prices, which snap back in January as selling pressure lifts. The effect is concentrated in the first 5 trading days of January and is magnified in high-December-selling-pressure environments. Keim (1983) documented small-cap January outperformance as partially explained by the tax-loss hypothesis; Reinganum (1983) confirmed.
